answer_raw:
PART 1: DECOMPOSE

Starting from the three 10-K reporting segments and decomposing where unit economics differ meaningfully:

Intelligent Cloud ($106.3B FY2025, +21%):
• Azure and AI cloud services: consumption-based compute, AI platform (Foundry), GitHub cloud — primary AI-demand capture unit; FY2025 +34%, Q2 FY2026 +39% CC
• GitHub developer platform: code repository + AI coding tools (Copilot); 4.7M paid subscribers; distinct high-switching-cost recurring revenue
• On-premises server products (SQL Server, Windows Server, Visual Studio): structural secular decline; transition unit generating bridge cash flows
• Enterprise and partner services: stable, slow-growth services

Productivity and Business Processes ($120.8B FY2025, +13%):
• M365 Commercial cloud: volume (450M+ seats) plus ARPU expansion via E5 and Copilot; largest subscription business
• M365 Commercial products (on-prem): structurally declining
• LinkedIn (~$17.8B FY2025): advertising plus professional subscriptions; cyclical, partially AI-enhanced
• Dynamics 365 (~$7.8B FY2025): ERP/CRM SaaS with embedded AI agents
• M365 Consumer cloud (~$7.4B FY2025): subscription-driven, ARPU-focused
• Dynamics on-premises: declining

More Personal Computing ($54.6B FY2025, +7%):
• Windows OEM (~$17.3B): PC market-tied; modest AI PC tailwind
• Gaming — Xbox content/services + hardware (~$23.5B): content/subscription driven, AI-neutral near-term
• Search and news advertising ex-TAC (~$13.9B): AI opportunity and substitution risk simultaneously

PART 2: APPLY ECONOMIC FRAMEWORKS PER UNIT

AZURE AND AI CLOUD SERVICES:
— Demand: AI creates unprecedented new compute demand for model training, inference, and agent execution. Azure grew 34% in FY2025 and 39% CC in Q2 FY2026. 'Demand continues to exceed available supply' (Hood, Q2 FY2026 earnings call). Hood disclosed that if all incoming GPU arrivals in Q1/Q2 FY2026 had been allocated solely to Azure, growth 'would have been over 40%' — the binding constraint is supply, not demand.
— Complementarity: AI workloads create pull-through for adjacent Azure services. Satya: 'The vast majority of Foundry customers use additional Azure solutions like developer services, app services, databases as they scale.' Agent 365 ('the first provider to offer this type of agent control plane across clouds') extends Azure's governance footprint to agent workloads on any cloud. Fabric ($2B+ annualized run rate, +60% YoY, 31,000+ customers) is an analytics pull-through. Each Copilot deployment generates inference calls billed through Azure, creating internal complementarity between PBP and Intelligent Cloud.
— Substitution: AWS, Google Cloud, and Oracle Cloud are competing AI infrastructure providers. Open-source models (DeepSeek, Qwen referenced by Nadella in the Q2 FY2026 call) reduce switching costs between cloud AI platforms by allowing customers to run models independently of the model provider relationship. Custom silicon from hyperscaler competitors may progressively reduce NVIDIA GPU dependency industry-wide.
— Supply constraints: Near-term moat (demand exceeds supply, providing pricing power and customer queues). Amy Hood's key disclosure on capital recovery: GPUs contracted for customers 'are sold for the entirety of their useful life' — converting supply risk into contracted revenue across a 6-year depreciation lifecycle. Maya 200 accelerator (30%+ TCO improvement vs. prior fleet, 10+ FLOPS at FP4 precision; Q2 FY2026 earnings call) and Cobalt 200 CPU (50%+ higher performance than first custom CPU) represent proprietary infrastructure advantages improving tokens-per-watt-per-dollar (Nadella's stated optimization metric), reducing cost-per-token over time as the fleet ages and matures.
— Assessment: Strongest AI-positive unit in the portfolio. Primary risk is OpenAI single-counterparty concentration: approximately 45% of the $625B commercial RPO is from OpenAI (Hood, Q2 FY2026). If OpenAI's Azure compute needs stabilize or diversify to other providers, Azure's growth rate would compress materially and the RPO metric would lose significant forward momentum.

GITHUB DEVELOPER PLATFORM:
— Demand: AI coding tools are the highest immediate AI productivity ROI category. Paid subscribers grew 75% YoY to 4.7M; Pro Plus individual subscriptions grew 77% sequentially (Q2 FY2026).
— Complementarity: GitHub AgentHQ orchestrates AI coding agents from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Cognition, and xAI in the context of customers' GitHub repositories — making GitHub the 'organizing layer regardless of model choice' (Nadella). Siemens deployed the full GitHub platform to 30,000+ developers. GitHub Copilot SDK allows developers to embed the same AI runtime into their own applications. WorkIQ as an MCP server layered with GitHub creates a bidirectional productivity loop between code and organizational knowledge.
— Substitution: Competing platforms (JetBrains AI, Cursor, Amazon CodeWhisperer) exist, but GitHub's network effects (largest enterprise code repository base, pull request history, CI/CD pipelines, issue tracking) create high switching costs.
— Assessment: Bullish. Enterprise code and development workflows are durable. Model-agnostic positioning reduces the risk that any single AI model's success displaces GitHub's control-plane role.

M365 COMMERCIAL CLOUD:
— Demand: AI creates incremental demand (Copilot as a paid add-on) layered on a stable, large subscription base, not replacing the base. Copilot seats grew 160%+ YoY to 15M (Q2 FY2026 earnings call).
— Complementarity: The M365 data layer (Teams, SharePoint, Exchange, OneDrive, calendar, contacts) is Copilot's proprietary grounding source. WorkIQ described as 'the most important database for any organization' by Nadella — accessing organizational tacit knowledge unavailable on competing platforms. Switching costs are elevated: any competitor must recreate this organizational data context from scratch. Copilot engagement is accelerating: daily active users up 10x YoY; conversations per user doubling YoY; largest deployment tripling (>35,000 seats per customer).
— Substitution: Google Workspace plus Gemini and AI-native productivity startups present real competitive pressure. However, the 450M+ installed base carries very high workflow integration and data gravity switching costs. [Assumption: if an AI-native competitor successfully replicates M365's organizational data network effects and workflow integrations, switching costs would decline materially.]
— Assessment: Bullish. At 15M Copilot seats of 450M+ commercial seats (approximately 3.3% penetration — this is a straightforward calculation from disclosed figures, not a management claim), the ARPU expansion opportunity is early-stage. [Assumption: if Copilot penetration reaches 20–30% of commercial seats over a 5-year horizon at a meaningful premium, the cumulative ARPU lift could add tens of billions in annual segment revenue, though specific per-seat economics are not disclosed in the target materials.]

LINKEDIN:
— Demand: Professional network; advertising and subscription revenues are labor-market sensitive. 'Double-digit member growth in LinkedIn with 30% growth in paid video ads' (Nadella, Q2 FY2026). FY2025 +9%; Q2 FY2026 +11% CC.
— Complementarity: AI-powered content recommendations, recruiter tools, and skills matching enhance engagement and ad product performance. Marketing Solutions outperforming in Q2 FY2026 suggests AI-enhanced ad product strength.
— Substitution: Lower immediate AI substitution risk than search; professional identity networks have high switching costs.
— Assessment: Moderate AI tailwind. Cyclical hiring market sensitivity more relevant than AI disruption risk over a 5-year horizon.

DYNAMICS 365:
— Demand: AI agents automating ERP/CRM workflows. Sandrik uses Microsoft's sales qualification agent to automate lead qualification; Weesa uses knowledge management agents (Nadella, Q2 FY2026). Dynamics 365 grew 19% CC in Q2 FY2026 across all workloads.
— Complementarity: Native AI agents in Dynamics increase retention (agent-embedded workflows create stickier product) and drive workload expansion.
— Substitution: Salesforce Agentforce, SAP, ServiceNow, Oracle — all pursuing overlapping agent strategies with similar bundling value propositions.
— Assessment: Positive but competition intense; Microsoft's M365 plus Dynamics plus Azure bundling advantage is the primary differentiator.

SEARCH AND NEWS ADVERTISING:
— Demand: AI is reshaping search. Copilot-enhanced Bing drove FY2025 search ex-TAC +20% via 'higher search volume and higher revenue per search' (MD&A).
— Complementarity: AI-enhanced search depth (more complex, longer queries → higher value-per-session).
— Substitution: Significant risk. AI chat interfaces (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) replace traditional search sessions for information retrieval queries. Sequential deceleration is already visible in disclosed data: search ex-TAC grew 20% in FY2025, 10% CC in Q2 FY2026, and is guided to 'high single digits' in Q3 FY2026 (Hood). 'Execution challenges' cited (Hood). [Assumption: if AI chat displaces a growing share of traditional search volume over 5 years, search advertising revenue growth would further decelerate absent offsetting volume gains or format innovation.]
— Key internal tension: Microsoft has invested $13B in OpenAI (Note 1, 10-K), the entity most likely to disrupt traditional search via ChatGPT. ChatGPT success generates Azure inference revenue (beneficial) but substitutes for Bing search sessions (harmful to search advertising). This is a structurally contradictory exposure within the same portfolio.
— Assessment: Mixed. Near-term AI tailwind from Bing Copilot; medium-to-long-term disruption risk from AI chat proliferation. Not a growth driver for the 5-year thesis.

SERVER PRODUCTS (ON-PREMISES):
— Demand: Declining due to cloud migration. FY2025 -3%; Q2 FY2026 +21% CC was anomalous (SQL Server 2025 launch, hybrid demand, pre-purchasing ahead of memory price increases — Hood cautioned 'could create additional volatility' in transactional purchasing).
— Assessment: Secular decline unit. Valuable transitional cash flow but not material to a 5-year AI thesis.

WINDOWS OEM:
— Demand: AI PC (Copilot+ PC) category could sustain a multi-year refresh cycle. Windows 11 reached 1 billion users in Q2 FY2026 (+45%+ YoY). However, Q3 FY2026 Windows OEM guided to decline 'roughly 10%' as the Windows 10 end-of-support benefit normalizes and elevated inventory works down.
— Complementarity: AI PCs require Windows (protects platform attachment); Copilot+ PCs represent a premium tier.
— Assessment: Modest AI tailwind from AI PC refresh cycle; not a material 5-year growth driver.

GAMING:
— Near-term evidence: Declined 9% CC in Q2 FY2026, 'below expectations driven by first-party content with impact across the platform' (Hood). Xbox hardware -25% in FY2025. Impairment charges taken.
— Complementarity: AI content generation could reduce development costs and personalize experiences — but no specific metrics are disclosed to support a quantified thesis.
— Assessment: Neutral to slightly negative near-term. Content execution risk is the primary variable, not AI dynamics.

PART 3: SYNTHESIZE

COHERENT MIXED THESIS (BULLISH ON AZURE AND M365 COPILOT; CAUTIOUS ON NEAR-TERM MARGINS; MIXED ON SEARCH):

Revenue trajectory (5-year horizon): Microsoft's AI-exposed units — Azure, M365 Copilot, GitHub Copilot, Dynamics 365 agents — constitute a growing share of total revenue and are each experiencing above-company-average growth. Azure at 39% CC is supply-constrained, meaning disclosed growth understates underlying demand. [Assumption: if global AI infrastructure adoption continues on its current trajectory and Microsoft's capacity additions come online as guided, Azure could sustain growth above 30% for 2–3 more years before compressing as the base scales.] M365 Copilot at approximately 3.3% penetration of 450M+ seats is early-stage; [Assumption: moderate penetration over 5 years at premium ARPU would be material to total company revenue, though per-seat pricing and steady-state penetration rates are not disclosed in the target materials.] GitHub Copilot's 75% YoY subscriber growth reflects deep enterprise adoption momentum. These units are sufficient to sustain overall company revenue growth in a range consistent with the 15–20% range observed in FY2025 and Q2 FY2026, barring exogenous shocks, as AI-driven units compound faster than the declining units (server products, on-prem Office) drag.

Margin trajectory (5-year horizon): Near-term gross margin compression is quantified and real. Microsoft Cloud gross margin is guided at approximately 65% for Q3 FY2026 versus 69% in FY2025. Hood provided the recovery mechanism: 'where you've sold the entirety of its life, the margins actually improve with time' — as AI infrastructure matures on a contracted revenue base, margins expand. Custom silicon (Maya 200, Cobalt 200) creates a proprietary path to better tokens-per-watt-per-dollar, progressively reducing cost-per-token as these assets scale. [Assumption: if AI software revenues (M365 Copilot, GitHub Copilot, Security Copilot) grow faster than AI infrastructure costs, the revenue mix shifts toward higher-margin software, gradually recovering blended gross margin toward or beyond pre-AI-buildout levels.] Management guided FY2026 full-year operating margins to be 'up slightly' versus FY2025's 45.6%, signaling management's expectation of a near-term inflection despite Q3 FY2026 guidance of 'down slightly.'

INTERNAL CONSISTENCY: The bullish Azure thesis and bullish M365 Copilot thesis are complementary, not contradictory — Copilot inference runs on Azure, increasing both segment revenues simultaneously. The mixed Search thesis does not contradict the Azure thesis because search advertising ($13.9B) is a small segment that generates minimal Azure demand. The declining server products thesis is already embedded in Intelligent Cloud's reported numbers (Azure's 34% growth masks a 3% server decline in FY2025; segment net growth was 21%). The OpenAI concentration risk is the key internal tension: if 45% of commercial RPO is OpenAI-sourced (disclosed by Hood, Q2 FY2026), a deceleration in OpenAI's Azure spend would compress both Azure's growth rate and the commercial RPO metric used to signal revenue visibility, creating a correlated downside scenario that the unit-level analysis alone would underweight.

KEY RISKS TO MONITOR (all grounded in disclosures):
1. OpenAI single-counterparty concentration: 45% of $625B commercial RPO from one partner (Hood, Q2 FY2026). Any strategic shift by OpenAI would materially impair the Azure growth narrative and commercial RPO trajectory.
2. Capital intensity versus demand timing: CapEx of $64.6B in FY2025 and $37.5B in Q2 FY2026 alone must be monetized across 6-year server/GPU lifecycles; $397B in total material cash requirements (MD&A) reflects multi-year fixed commitments.
3. Gross margin trough duration: Current guidance of ~65% cloud gross margin in Q3 FY2026 may persist longer or at a lower level if AI infrastructure scaling requires investment beyond current guidance horizons.
4. AI-native productivity substitution: If AI-native competitors successfully replicate M365's organizational data integration, ARPU expansion from Copilot could underperform [Assumption explicitly labeled — this is a risk, not a current disclosed trend].
5. Regulatory risk: EU AI Act (referenced in Risk Factors), export controls (OBBBA assessment underway per 10-K MD&A income taxes section), and competition authority scrutiny (referenced in Risk Factors across multiple jurisdictions) could restrict AI product deployment in key markets.
6. IRS tax contingency: $28.9B proposed additional tax assessment plus penalties and interest remains unresolved (Note 11); represents material tail risk to net income.
